_provenance.scope: greenfield brand extraction — synthesized from BRAND.md, no source URL

site.name: Chapitô
site.tagline: Molhos com piruetas. Casa pequena, picante grande.
site.place: Costa do Castelo, Alfama, Lisbon, Portugal + Estremoz, Alentejo (pepper patch)

palette.role: dominant brand surface ground (peach-coral / cravo, also the April Revolution flower)
secondary light surface (sand at Caparica beach); sunburst alt-ray fill; stripe accent
primary loud orange (piri-piri ripe-red-orange); PIRI-PIRI family section ground
turquoise teal accent (Lisbon tile / Tejo morning); highlighter + polka-dot + chip half-band
yolk mustard; DOCE FOGO family section ground; highlighter accent
brick / burgundy (ripe-malagueta-chili); ticket-stub border; highlighter accent; body emphasis
deep teal-navy (Tagus at dusk); CABEÇUDO ground; big-dark bottle hero ground; circle CTA fill
limewash white (never pure #fff); marquee tape; light text on dark grounds; persistent-bottom-bar ground

logotype-medallion.loadBearingRule: wordmark medallion only — never standing text

mono.loadBearingRule: ticket-stubs, micro-labels, dates, addresses, numerals; ≤ 14px load-bearing

logo.differentiationFromReference: the reference (opificiocattaneo.com) uses a circular medallion; Chapitô deliberately rejects the medallion form to keep the logo identity-specific. Tent silhouette as frame is the load-bearing replacement.
logo.synthesizedBasis: A flat, hand-printed-feel logo of a circus big-top tent silhouette, viewed straight-on. Triangular tent with three vertical stripes inside (alternating cravo + cal), thin noite outline, scalloped malagueta base flap. A small pennant flag with a triangular cutout (echoing the ô circumflex) rises from the apex of the tent on a thin pole. The word CHAPITÔ is set inside the tent in bold condensed all-caps Anton, noite. Sub-line below the tent in mono: MOLHOS · À MÃO · LISBOA · 2018. Two small star ornaments frame the wordmark. Two color variants: noite on cravo; cal on tejo.
logo.pendingExternalGen: Gemini 3 Pro Image Preview — see BRAND.md § Logo direction prompt seed

imageryLanes.description: Cutout B&W vintage photography (LOAD-BEARING). Black-and-white photographs of Portuguese street life and mid-century fairground archives — sardine fishermen, Alfama-balcony grandmothers, Lisbon tram drivers, Caparica beach photographers, vintage travelling-circus performers. Cut out from their backgrounds (transparent surround) and floated over solid-color section grounds. The cutout-collage register replaces the cinematic-landscape lane most artisan brands default to.

Hand-painted bottle labels. Each product carries a hand-painted, screen-printed-look label in palette tones — bold flat areas, hand-cut typography (raster, not web), one signature circus-figure illustration per product.

Workshop process + Alentejo patch photography (used sparingly). Color photographs — copper pot, curing shed, pepper patch in late summer, hands at work, jars in the cooling rack. Used only on about + process pages. NEVER in home color-block product sections.

antiReferences: Italian liquor-maker pastiche (Opificio / Liquoreria / Amaro / Aperitivo / Cattaneo) — the reference brand is the visual register, not a source of words or category
Slow Food / Cereal-Magazine / Kinfolk country-luxe (terracotta + sage + linen)
Tourist-Lisbon clichés (azulejo as decoration, fado, saudade, pastel de nata, bacalhau as set-dressing)
Etsy folk-craft / homestead-Americana (mason jars, kraft paper, chalkboards)
Brooklyn industrial-craft (Edison bulb, subway tile, Helvetica-on-blackboard)
Editorial-register vocabulary in chrome (atelier, the journal, dispatches)
Script web fonts as standing text
Italic-classical serif as display (that is High Lonesome's register)
directionPhraseCandidates: big-top fairground poster wrapping a quiet pepper farm. Cutout-collage over solid grounds. Loud, painted, off-axis, Portuguese.
Portuguese hot sauces dressed as a 1962 travelling-circus poster. Alentejo slow-cook, Alfama big-top spectacle.
fairground-marquee maximalism in service of single-maker Portuguese hot sauces. Drenched palette, cutout collage, native Portuguese voice.
